subject: The right way to dry out a Cape Cod house in July
preheader: Dehumidifier settings, basement rules, and why a colder thermostat isn't the fix.
title: Keeping the Cape's damp out of your house
introHtml: It's mid-July, the Sound is warm, and every breeze carries water. Cape Cod houses don't just get hot in summer — they get damp, and damp is what makes rooms feel sticky, basements smell musty, and AC bills climb. Here's how moisture actually gets in, and how to send it back out.
articleHtml:
Cape Cod sits in the ocean's breath. Sea air carries more water than inland air, our water table runs high, and summer wind pushes humidity into every gap in the shell. Managing that moisture is the cheapest comfort upgrade there is: dry air at 76 degrees feels better than damp air at 72, which means less AC runtime — and that's what energy efficiency looks like in July.

Make your AC earn its keep as a dehumidifier

  • Fan on "auto," never "on." Between cooling cycles, a constantly running fan re-evaporates the water sitting on the cold coil and blows it right back into the house.
  • Longer, slower cycles dry better. An oversized unit blasts cold air and shuts off before it wrings moisture out. Frequent short cycles are a sign worth investigating.
  • Target 50 percent relative humidity. An inexpensive hygrometer tells you where you stand; below 50, mildew and dust mites struggle to get going.

Basements and crawl spaces: the damp engine

Cool below-grade surfaces are condensation magnets. Opening basement windows on a muggy day feeds them warm, wet air — keep them shut. Run a dehumidifier set to 50 percent with a hose to a drain, so it never sits full and shut off. Dirt crawl spaces need a ground vapor barrier. And if you see white mineral bloom on the foundation or the sill smells earthy, water is moving through the structure — that's a repair job, not a thermostat setting.

Humidity control is energy work.dcbuildersofcapecod.com/blog">our blog.
tips: Set the AC fan to "auto" — a fan that runs nonstop pushes coil moisture back into your rooms.
Keep basement windows closed on muggy days; that "fresh air" condenses on cool concrete.
Confirm bath fans and the dryer vent exhaust outdoors — not into the attic or crawl space.
ctaLine: If your basement smells musty or the AC can't keep up, reply with a photo or call Dave at (508) 566-1190 — consultations are free and estimates come within 24 hours.
